import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

transcript.text: I’ve got the data model and the real page flows now, including checkout navigation and the cart/order Redux transitions. I’m moving into implementation: first the shared Storybook runtime and MSW handlers, then story files in descending order of reuse so the later page stories can lean on the same environment.

transcript.aggregated_output:
import { TypedUseSelectorHook, useDispatch, useSelector } from 'react-redux'

import { AppDispatch, RootState } from './store'

/**
 * The reason we expose our own dispatch and selector
 * is that we get typescript safety anywhere, which is
 * automatically updated as we add more slices to the application.
 */

export const useAppDispatch = () => useDispatch<AppDispatch>()
export const useAppSelector: TypedUseSelectorHook<RootState> = useSelector

export * from './store'
export * from './hooks'

import { createSelector } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'

import { RootState } from '../store'

const selectOrder = (state: RootState) => state.order

export const selectOrderItems = createSelector([selectOrder], (order) => order.items)

export const selectOrderTotal = createSelector([selectOrderItems], (items) =>
  items.reduce((acc, item) => acc + item.quantity * item.price, 0)
)

I’m shifting to story creation now, starting with low-level reusable components so I can validate the shared runtime before leaning on it for the page stories.

I’m writing the first story file against the real `CategoryList` and `CategoriesSection` usage patterns: same category stub data, same router links around the component, and the same rounded vs square variants. Right after that I’ll run the Storybook Vitest project just for this file and fix anything it exposes before moving on.

transcript.text: The second story flushed out a real test-runner issue rather than a component bug: Vite is re-optimizing `react-loading-skeleton` mid-run, which breaks the Storybook browser test module graph. I’m fixing that in config so the rest of the story test loop stays stable, then I’ll rerun this file before adding anything else.

I’m patching Vite’s prebundle list now so Storybook browser tests don’t get reloaded the first time a component imports one of these UI dependencies. After that I’ll rerun the restaurant card stories and only continue once the file is clean.

I’m editing the restaurant detail stories now with one explicit portal check. The goal is to prove the modal root and shared runtime really work, not just that the page text shows up.

transcript.text: The last failures were both assertion mismatches again: the 404 page has two “Home” buttons in scope, and the modal correctly initialized from a quantity of one. I’m adjusting the story to preload the exact quantity I want and to assert the error state more precisely, then I’ll rerun it.

The next story file is checkout, and I’m keeping it grounded in the real user flow rather than building a fake harness around the form. I’ll cover the loaded page, the empty-order variant, and the validation branch by driving the real inputs and buttons in `play`.

TypeScript is clean now. I’m doing one last consolidated Storybook Vitest run over the story files we added so the final state reflects the current preview and story code together, not just the earlier per-file checkpoints.

import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

content.text: The runtime shape is clearer now: React Router + Redux + styled-components theme, with global CSS injected through `GlobalStyle`, axios-based data hooks, and a portal target named `#modal` already embedded by the app routes. I’m scanning for additional browser-state reads and real page/component compositions so the shared preview can cover them once instead of patching story by story.

content.text: I’ve got the data model and the real page flows now, including checkout navigation and the cart/order Redux transitions. I’m moving into implementation: first the shared Storybook runtime and MSW handlers, then story files in descending order of reuse so the later page stories can lean on the same environment.

import { TypedUseSelectorHook, useDispatch, useSelector } from 'react-redux'

import { AppDispatch, RootState } from './store'

/**
 * The reason we expose our own dispatch and selector
 * is that we get typescript safety anywhere, which is
 * automatically updated as we add more slices to the application.
 */

export const useAppDispatch = () => useDispatch<AppDispatch>()
export const useAppSelector: TypedUseSelectorHook<RootState> = useSelector

Exit code: 0

export * from './store'
export * from './hooks'

Exit code: 0

import { createSelector } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'

import { RootState } from '../store'

const selectOrder = (state: RootState) => state.order

export const selectOrderItems = createSelector([selectOrder], (order) => order.items)

export const selectOrderTotal = createSelector([selectOrderItems], (items) =>
  items.reduce((acc, item) => acc + item.quantity * item.price, 0)
)

content.text: The second story flushed out a real test-runner issue rather than a component bug: Vite is re-optimizing `react-loading-skeleton` mid-run, which breaks the Storybook browser test module graph. I’m fixing that in config so the rest of the story test loop stays stable, then I’ll rerun this file before adding anything else.

I’m patching Vite’s prebundle list now so Storybook browser tests don’t get reloaded the first time a component imports one of these UI dependencies. After that I’ll rerun the restaurant card stories and only continue once the file is clean.

content.text: TypeScript caught a few Storybook typing quirks rather than runtime issues: the preview store helper can be simpler, and a few component stories need default `args` because their components have required props. I’m patching those now and then rerunning `tsc` to make sure the whole setup is clean.

content.text:
Storybook is now wired to the app’s real runtime patterns without changing app code.

I set the default environment in [`.storybook/preview.tsx`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/.storybook/preview.tsx), with the same core runtime pieces the app uses: router, Redux store, styled-components theme, and `GlobalStyle`. I also added global MSW handlers in [`.storybook/msw-handlers.ts`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/.storybook/msw-handlers.ts), served `public` from [`.storybook/main.ts`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/.storybook/main.ts), and created the modal portal root in [`.storybook/preview-body.html`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/.storybook/preview-body.html). I added `msw-storybook-addon` and expanded Vite prebundling in [`vite.config.ts`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/vite.config.ts) so browser-mode Storybook tests stay stable.

I added 10 story files with play assertions copied from real page and component usage:
[Category.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/components/Category/Category.stories.tsx), [RestaurantCard.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/components/RestaurantCard/RestaurantCard.stories.tsx), [Header.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/components/Header/Header.stories.tsx), [ShoppingCartMenu.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/components/ShoppingCartMenu/ShoppingCartMenu.stories.tsx), [HomePage.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/pages/HomePage/HomePage.stories.tsx), [CategoryListPage.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/pages/CategoryListPage/CategoryListPage.stories.tsx), [CategoryDetailPage.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/pages/CategoryDetailPage/CategoryDetailPage.stories.tsx), [RestaurantDetailPage.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/pages/RestaurantDetailPage/RestaurantDetailPage.stories.tsx), [CheckoutPage.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/pages/CheckoutPage/CheckoutPage.stories.tsx), and [SuccessPage.stories.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T18-33-30Z-67c22998/project/src/pages/SuccessPage/SuccessPage.stories.tsx).
